content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Daria Nepriakhina 🇺🇦 on UnsplashA few years ago, when someone said ‘UX’, we automatically thought of wireframes, journeys, and user testing. That was the heart of digital design. It still matters today, but it stopped being enough on its own. Something changed, and it wasn’t just the technology. What changed is how people relate to anything digital in the first place.
We’ve entered what I’d call a post-traditional-UX era. And the AI isn’t the only thing to blame. There’s also a new kind of user expectation now: more immediate, more personalized, harder to pin down.
The trap of UX as a checklist
In a lot of companies, UX turned into a set of deliverables: ‘run some interviews’, ‘sketch a few wireframes’ or ‘test it with three users and call it done’. Sound familiar? It does to me too. And to be clear, none of that is wrong on its own. The problem is that it became mechanical. As if it were a recipe that works for anything you throw at it.
But designing experiences was never about following a formula. It’s about deeply understanding the context, the intention, and the emotional dynamic of whoever is using what we build. And that got a lot more complex once AI entered the picture.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Node-Link Diagram of The Privacy Framework and Controls by CSF ToolsFirst, we designed interfaces. Then we designed flows. Now we’re designing systems that adapt on their own, that predict, that hold conversations, that reconfigure themselves. AI doesn’t just perform tasks anymore; it makes decisions. And that changes what “designing an experience” even means.
A simple example: before, if someone searched for a product on an online store and couldn’t find it, it was in the search bar or in the flow. Today, if an AI shows a ‘smart’ result that isn’t actually relevant, whose fault is that? The design? The algorithm? The prompt?
The line between design, content, system, and algorithmic logic is getting blurry. And designers have to get comfortable moving around in that blur because it’s not going away.
UX now means designing living systems
When we design an experience mediated by AI, we’re no longer just thinking about screens. We’re thinking about how a system behaves across thousands of different users, in real time, making decisions we don’t directly control.
That means ‘the average user’ or a straight linear flow isn’t enough to design around anymore. What we actually need to understand is:

• How to design for ambiguity — what happens when the AI gets it wrong

• How to set ethical and visual limits on how much autonomy the system gets

• How to explain to the user what’s happening, without overloading them

• How to iterate on something that changes every single time it’s used
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Luke Chesser on UnsplashSo what do we actually do about it
I’m not saying we stop doing UX. Quite the opposite. But it needs rethinking, and by now this isn’t some radical discovery — it’s just where the baseline has to sit. A few things that work for me:

• Design prompts the way you design interfaces. If you work with generative AI, prompts are your new Figma file. Treat them with the same care, not as an afterthought.

• Work closer to data and engineering. Design stopped being only visual a while ago. It’s structural now. Your job starts where the interface used to end.

• Prototype with AI, not just for it. Use it to explore directions you wouldn’t have landed on alone. Try the weird options. That’s the point of prototyping in the first place.

• Train your team to spot invisible friction. AI can look like magic, but it creates new kinds of frustration too. Users don’t always know how to name it, but they feel it.

• Bring ethical thinking into the design process itself. Ask ‘what happens if this gets misread’, ‘who does this leave out’, and ‘what happens if the AI gets it wrong here’ as part of the work, not as a footnote.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Amélie Mourichon on UnsplashThe future isn’t a future without designers
There’s a fear, and a fair one, that AI will take over part of our job. It might. But it also opens up new territory: designing how technology makes decisions on our behalf.
That’s exactly the territory where design has the most to offer — from how we present alternatives, to how we give feedback, to how we structure a system people can actually trust.
UX didn’t die. It just stopped living only in heatmaps and onboarding flows. Now it lives in the invisible parts, in what’s dynamic, in what keeps changing.
And that’s exactly what makes it genuinely challenging. And exciting.
